title: Academic Advising
summary: Advisors help you read DARS, pick classes, and make sure your plan still points toward graduation.

modes.label: In person
modes.summary: Book an advising appointment, arrive a few minutes early, and talk through your schedule face to face.

steps.title: Book the advising appointment
steps.description: Start in MyASU or your college advising page and choose the appointment reason that fits your question.
bullets: Open your advising link from MyASU or the ASU advising page.
Choose a reason like schedule planning, DARS questions, or registration help.
Select an in-person slot and save the confirmation details.

visual.title: MyASU and the advising scheduler
visual.caption: You are usually choosing a reason, a date, and whether it is in person or virtual.

steps.title: Arrive and check in
steps.description: When you get there, the process is usually simple: check in, wait briefly, and have your questions ready.
bullets: Bring your ASU ID and have your MyASU or DARS open if you can.
Tell the front desk your name and that you have an advising appointment.
Use the waiting time to write the first two things you want answered.
steps.reassurance: Hundreds of students do this every week. You do not need to sound polished to belong in the room.

visual.title: Check-in desk and waiting area
visual.caption: It usually feels like an office suite, not a test.

steps.title: Use the meeting to get clarity, not just facts
steps.description: The advisor will usually pull up your record, your DARS, and the next set of decisions in front of you.
bullets: Ask them to explain what they are looking at on your DARS.
Take notes when they mention deadlines, holds, or sequencing.
Before you leave, repeat back your next classes or next tasks in your own words.

visual.title: DARS, schedule, and next semester planning
visual.caption: The biggest win is leaving with fewer unknowns than when you walked in.

modes.label: Online
modes.summary: Join a virtual advising appointment from your laptop with MyASU and DARS already open.

steps.title: Choose an online advising slot
steps.description: Pick the online appointment option if that is the fastest or easiest way to get seen.
bullets: Book through MyASU or your college advising site.
Confirm whether the meeting is Zoom, phone, or another platform.
Save the link and the appointment time immediately.

visual.title: Virtual meeting link and MyASU tabs
visual.caption: Having DARS and your class list open before the call saves a lot of time.

steps.title: Prep the screen before the meeting starts
steps.description: Treat the first five minutes like setup time so you are not searching for tabs while the advisor waits.
bullets: Open DARS, your current schedule, and the notes app you will use.
Write down your main question in one sentence.
Join a few minutes early if the appointment has a meeting link.
steps.reassurance: If you are new to advising, it is completely normal to start with a broad question like “Can you help me understand what I am looking at?”

visual.title: DARS on one tab, notes on another
visual.caption: The goal is to be ready to follow the advisor’s screen-sharing or instructions.

steps.title: End with written next steps
steps.description: Virtual meetings go fast. Make sure you leave with a written list of what to do next.
bullets: Ask the advisor to repeat the next step if needed.
Write down course numbers, deadlines, and any holds they mention.
Check your email after the meeting for appointment notes or follow-up links.

visual.title: One clean list of next actions
visual.caption: Even a short advising appointment is useful if you leave with a concrete list.

modes.label: Drop-in
modes.summary: Use drop-in advising or quick advising hours when you need a short answer or the next available person.

steps.title: Check for drop-in or express advising hours
steps.description: Some colleges offer quick advising or same-day support for shorter questions.
bullets: Look for drop-in, express, or same-day advising on your college site.
Double-check whether it is in person or a virtual queue.
Write your question down so you use the short visit well.

visual.title: Quick advising for one focused question
visual.caption: Drop-in is often best for one clear question, not a full semester plan.

steps.title: Lead with the one thing you most need answered
steps.description: Start with your most urgent question instead of the full backstory.
bullets: Try: “I need help understanding what to take next semester.”
Show them your DARS or class list if they ask.
If the question is bigger than drop-in can cover, ask how to book a full appointment.
steps.reassurance: You are allowed to use drop-in even if you feel like your question is obvious or basic.

visual.title: Short question, direct answer
visual.caption: The clearer your first question is, the more useful the short visit becomes.

steps.title: Turn the answer into a follow-up plan
steps.description: If the question opens up bigger issues, leave knowing what the next advising step should be.
bullets: Ask if you should schedule a longer follow-up appointment.
Write down any dates, forms, or holds they mention.
Check MyASU the same day so nothing slips.

visual.title: Quick help should still move you forward
visual.caption: The goal is not perfection. The goal is one less unknown.

questionSet.defaultOpener: Hi, I want to make sure I understand what classes and next steps make sense for me right now.
questionSet.defaultProTip: Open DARS before the meeting if you can. Even if you do not understand it, having it on the screen helps the conversation move faster.
questionSet.firstTimeIntro: If this is your first advising visit, let yourself be a beginner. Advisors answer “basic” questions all day.

scenarioPlaceholders.class: I am not sure if I am in the right classes and my DARS is confusing me.
scenarioPlaceholders.money: I am worried about paying for school and I do not know what deadlines or tasks I should be watching.
scenarioPlaceholders.overwhelmed: I feel behind already and I need help figuring out what to handle first.
scenarioPlaceholders.schedule: I do not know what classes I should take next semester or if I am on track.
scenarioPlaceholders.something-else: I know I need advising, but I am not sure how to explain my question yet.

first-year: Am I in the right classes for my first semester?
Can you show me how to read the most important part of my DARS?
What should I be paying attention to before registration opens again?
Is there anything on my account or timeline I should be worried about now?
What is one thing first-year students usually miss that I should know about?
second-year: Am I building the right sequence for my major requirements?
Are there any prerequisites I should plan around now?
What classes would you recommend next semester and why?
Is my DARS showing any warning signs yet?
What deadlines should I put in my calendar today?
third-year: Am I still on track to graduate on time?
Are there any bottleneck classes or requirements I need to plan around?
Is there anything in my DARS or progress that needs attention this semester?
Should I be thinking about internships, upper-division requirements, or graduation timing right now?
What should I do next if I need a bigger schedule change?
fourth-year-plus: Am I missing any graduation requirements or deadlines?
What should I verify now so my final semesters go smoothly?
Are there any holds, petitions, or approvals that could delay graduation?
What classes or requirements matter most from this point forward?
What is the smartest next step if my timeline has changed?
